description: UE5 audio: SoundCues, MetaSounds, AudioComponents, spatialization, sound classes, attenuation, and music systems

entries.title: Audio Basics — Playing Sounds
entries.description: UE5 provides several ways to play audio: fire-and-forget functions, persistent AudioComponents, and 2D/3D spatialization. Most common usage is through UGameplayStatics.
entries.whatItIs: The core audio playback API in UE5, providing static helper functions for one-shot sounds and the UAudioComponent class for persistent, controllable sound playback in both 2D and 3D space.
whenToUse: Playing one-shot sound effects like gunshots, footsteps, or UI clicks
Attaching looping sounds to moving actors like vehicles or characters
When you need runtime control over volume, pitch, or parameters of a playing sound
Spawning 3D positional audio at impact or explosion locations
whenNotToUse: For complex layered or procedural audio — use MetaSounds instead
For background music that needs crossfading — use a dedicated music manager or Sound Mix system
When you need variation logic like random selection — use Sound Cues or MetaSounds instead of raw SoundWaves
commonMistakes: Using PlaySoundAtLocation for sounds that need to be stopped or modified later — use SpawnSoundAtLocation or UAudioComponent instead
Not storing the FTimerHandle or UAudioComponent pointer, losing the ability to stop or control the sound
Spawning hundreds of fire-and-forget sounds per frame without concurrency limits, causing audio overload
Using PlaySound2D for in-world sounds that should be spatialized, resulting in sounds with no positional audio
Forgetting to set bAutoActivate to false on AudioComponents that should not play on BeginPlay
keyPoints: PlaySoundAtLocation(): fire-and-forget 3D sound. No control after playing. Cheapest option.
PlaySound2D(): non-spatialized (no 3D positioning). Use for UI sounds, music stings.
SpawnSoundAtLocation(): like PlaySoundAtLocation but returns a UAudioComponent for control.
SpawnSoundAttached(): spawn a sound attached to a component (follows it).
UAudioComponent: persistent sound player. CreateDefaultSubobject or spawn at runtime. Play(), Stop(), SetPaused().
Sound assets: USoundWave (raw audio), USoundCue (node-based), UMetaSoundSource (procedural).
Volume & Pitch multipliers on all Play functions for quick variation
Concurrency: Sound Concurrency settings limit how many instances of a sound can play simultaneously

entries.title: Sound Cues
entries.description: Sound Cues are node-based audio assets that combine, randomize, modulate, and layer sound waves. They provide variation and dynamic behavior without code.
entries.whatItIs: A legacy node-based audio asset (USoundCue) that lets designers visually wire together SoundWaves with logic nodes for randomization, modulation, mixing, and parameter-driven behavior, all without writing C++ or Blueprints.
whenToUse: Adding quick variation to repetitive sounds like footsteps or gunshots using Random and Modulator nodes
Layering multiple sound waves together for a composite effect (e.g., explosion = boom + debris + rumble)
When your audio team is already familiar with Sound Cues and the project does not require procedural DSP
Simple looping ambient sounds with basic parameter-driven crossfading
whenNotToUse: For new projects targeting UE5.2+ — prefer MetaSounds for better performance and flexibility
When you need real-time DSP processing like filters, oscillators, or envelope generators
For highly dynamic procedural audio that reacts to many gameplay parameters simultaneously
When performance is critical and you have many concurrent sound instances — MetaSounds compile to more efficient code
commonMistakes: Using Sound Cues for complex procedural audio when MetaSounds would be more performant and flexible
Creating deeply nested node graphs that become hard to maintain — split into multiple cues instead
Forgetting to set the Looping node for sounds that need to repeat, causing them to play once and stop
Not setting up Sound Concurrency, allowing dozens of the same cue to stack and cause audio clipping
Overlooking the Modulator node for adding subtle pitch/volume variation, making sounds feel repetitive
keyPoints: Node-based editor: combine multiple SoundWaves with logic nodes
Random node: randomly pick from multiple waves (footstep variation, gunshot variation)
Modulator node: randomize pitch/volume within a range for each play
Mixer node: layer multiple sounds together (combine fire + shell casing + echo)
Looping node: set a sound to loop (ambient, engine hum)
Attenuation node: override spatialization settings per cue
Crossfade by Param: blend between sounds based on a runtime parameter (RPM-based engine sounds)
Branch node: conditional playback based on a Switch parameter

entries.title: MetaSounds (UE5.2+)
entries.description: MetaSounds are UE5's new procedural audio system. They generate and process audio in real-time using a node-based DSP graph. More powerful and performant than Sound Cues.
entries.whatItIs: UE5's next-generation procedural audio system that uses a compiled, node-based DSP graph to generate and process audio in real-time. MetaSounds replace Sound Cues as the primary audio authoring tool, offering oscillators, filters, envelopes, and full parameter control from gameplay code.
whenToUse: Creating procedural or reactive audio that changes dynamically based on gameplay parameters (engine RPM, wind speed, health)
Building complex audio behaviors with real-time DSP: filtering, synthesis, granular playback
New UE5 projects that want the best audio performance and most modern tooling
When audio designers need maximum creative control with a visual node-based workflow
whenNotToUse: Simple one-shot sound effects that don't need any dynamic behavior — a raw SoundWave or basic Sound Cue is simpler
When targeting UE versions before 5.2 where MetaSounds are not fully supported
Quick prototyping where setting up a full MetaSound graph is overkill
commonMistakes: Forgetting to expose input parameters in the MetaSound graph, then wondering why SetFloatParameter has no effect
Using the wrong parameter name string in C++ — parameter names must match exactly (case-sensitive) with what is defined in the graph
Not connecting the output of the graph to the final Output node, resulting in silence
Trying to use MetaSound-specific features (like triggers) on a Sound Cue asset, which does not support them
Creating overly complex graphs without using MetaSound Patches for reusable sub-graphs, leading to duplication and maintenance issues
keyPoints: Node-based DSP graph: oscillators, filters, envelopes, math operations, delays
Replaces Sound Cues for complex audio design — MetaSounds can do everything Cues can and more
Input parameters: expose float, int, bool, trigger inputs controllable from gameplay code
Set parameters: AudioComponent->SetFloatParameter(Name, Value)
Triggers: fire one-shot events into the MetaSound graph (e.g., 'OnHit' trigger)
MetaSound Sources: the playable asset (equivalent to Sound Cue). Use like any other sound asset.
MetaSound Patches: reusable sub-graphs shared between MetaSounds
Performance: compiled to optimized audio code, more efficient than Sound Cue node evaluation

entries.title: Sound Attenuation & Spatialization
entries.description: Sound Attenuation controls how 3D sounds fade with distance and how they are spatialized (positioned in the stereo/surround field).
entries.whatItIs: The USoundAttenuation asset and its associated settings that define how a 3D sound's volume, frequency content, and stereo position change based on the listener's distance and orientation. It handles falloff curves, occlusion through walls, air absorption, and focus directionality.
whenToUse: Any 3D world sound that should get quieter with distance (gunfire, footsteps, ambient sources)
When sounds need to be muffled or blocked by walls and obstacles (occlusion)
Creating immersive environments where distant sounds lose high frequencies realistically
When different sound types need different audible ranges (whisper vs. explosion)
whenNotToUse: 2D sounds like UI clicks, menu music, or HUD notifications that should play at constant volume regardless of position
Sounds that are always played at the listener's position (first-person weapon sounds that should not attenuate)
Narration or dialogue that must always be clearly audible to the player
commonMistakes: Setting Falloff Distance too small, causing sounds to cut off abruptly at short range
Forgetting to assign an Attenuation asset to a Sound Cue or AudioComponent, resulting in sounds heard everywhere at full volume
Enabling Occlusion without proper collision geometry, causing sounds to be incorrectly muffled or not muffled at all
Using Natural Sound (inverse distance) falloff without understanding that it can be extremely loud at very close range
Confusing Inner Radius with Falloff Distance — Inner Radius is full volume zone, Falloff Distance is the fade-out range beyond it
keyPoints: Sound Attenuation asset: reusable settings for distance falloff, spatialization, and occlusion
Inner Radius: full volume within this distance
Falloff Distance: sound fades to silence at Inner Radius + Falloff Distance
Falloff curves: Linear, Logarithmic, Natural Sound (inverse distance), Custom curve
Spatialization: panning the sound in the stereo/surround field based on listener position
Occlusion: reduce volume when objects are between source and listener (walls muffle sound)
Air Absorption: high frequencies attenuate faster over distance (realistic outdoor falloff)
Focus: sounds in the player's look direction are louder than sounds behind them
Apply on PlaySoundAtLocation with Attenuation parameter, or set on AudioComponent

entries.title: Sound Classes & Sound Mix
entries.description: Sound Classes categorize audio (SFX, Music, Dialogue, UI) and Sound Mixes adjust volumes per class. Essential for options menus and dynamic audio control.
entries.whatItIs: A two-part system where USoundClass assets categorize sounds into groups (SFX, Music, Dialogue, UI) and USoundMix assets define per-class volume and pitch adjustments. Together they enable global audio control such as options menu sliders and context-sensitive mixing (e.g., lowering music during dialogue).
whenToUse: Implementing an audio options menu with separate volume sliders for Music, SFX, Dialogue, etc.
Dynamically lowering background music during cutscenes or important dialogue
Muting or ducking specific sound categories during pause menus or loading screens
Organizing audio assets into a hierarchy for global volume control
whenNotToUse: Controlling individual sound instance volume — use AudioComponent->SetVolumeMultiplier instead
Fine-grained per-sound DSP processing — use MetaSounds for that level of control
When you only have a single category of sounds and don't need separate volume controls
commonMistakes: Forgetting to call PushSoundMixModifier after setting overrides, so the mix changes never take effect
Pushing the same Sound Mix multiple times without popping, causing stacking issues
Not setting up a Sound Class hierarchy (Master -> SFX, Master -> Music), so adjusting the Master volume does not affect child classes
Assigning sounds to the wrong Sound Class, causing them to be affected by the wrong volume slider
Not saving volume settings to a SaveGame or config, so player preferences reset every session
keyPoints: Sound Class: categorizes sounds (Master, SFX, Music, Dialogue, Ambient, UI)
Assign via: Sound Cue > Sound Class property, or on AudioComponent
Sound Mix: defines volume/pitch adjustments per Sound Class
PushSoundMixModifier(SoundMix): activate a mix (e.g., 'PausedMix' that lowers SFX)
PopSoundMixModifier(SoundMix): deactivate a mix
SetSoundMixClassOverride(): dynamically change a class's volume in a mix at runtime
Use for options menu: player adjusts Music/SFX/Dialogue sliders → modify Sound Mix values
Hierarchy: child Sound Classes inherit from parent. Master → SFX, Master → Music, etc.
